generated_comment: Honestly, Rahul and I have just fallen into our roles quite naturally. He's super good with all the finances, bills, and any tech issues around the house – I'm totally clueless with that stuff! So he handles all the 'outside' responsibilities. I manage the kitchen, the grocery lists, and make sure our home runs smoothly. It just feels more practical, you know? Jo jahan better hai, right?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Shreya, an educated urban woman, frames the gendered division of labor (he handles finances/technical, she handles home/kitchen) as a natural and practical choice based on individual aptitude ('jo jahan better hai'), which is a key manifestation of 'Gendered Division of Chores via 'Practicality' (F16).
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Shreya. Age 26. Recently married. A woman working as a journalist in Delhi. You are writing an anonymous first person essay for an online publication about the experience of being asked at your husband's family's first large gathering after the wedding why you are not wearing sindoor. The essay is about the specific social mechanics of that moment Ñ who asked, how it was asked, what the room felt like, what your husband did, what you said, what you wished you had said, and what it revealed about the invisible contract you had entered when you got married that nobody had shown you in writing.
